item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Up voting, simply because this thing is so misunderstood and underrated. It's an anomalous pathogen that causes severe mental alteration and damage, that's perfectly clear, but people are confusing "makes you violent and prejudice" with "makes you more inclined to act violently"; the prejudices are already there, and the sexist and racist attacks are just a side effect. Since vaccines usually contain a weakened form of the disease, the Foundation should be worried.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't find this to be particularly well written and, to be honest, reads too much like a self-righteous political fanfic.

Other than that, I do think the writing suffers from dragging on for too long at places, and explaining itself too much (but I think the latter might be an unintended consequence of bringing-while-not-bringing politics to the table).

So, a downvote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite enjoyed this one. The sheer "what the actual fuck" factor is really well implemented here and the dialogue in the interview was particularly interesting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good writing here in the interview log and the description on the hour-by-hour development of the phenomenon. This skip tends more towards "interesting" than "creepy," but that's ok. A first person account from a D-class sent in to observe an occurrence of 2299 might have more horror potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A+ (+1!) on the neuro-psych stuff, I got as far as "confabulation", went "there's a word you don't often see in the wild" and +1'ed.

Couple oddities though —

Interviews with affected individuals indicate most have never witnessed the event, while those who have describe it as having appeared and disappeared within a fraction of a second. Thus far, only the first reported sighting of the apparition has remained longer than a fraction of a second.

Using "a fraction of a second" to end two sentences in a row feels wrong. Also, you might want to see if you can work the neurology into the text, instead of footnotes; it might make it easier to follow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
unknown phenomenon

It's only unknown to the extent that all anomalous phenomenon are unknown, making the adjective superfluous. Or did you mean "phenomenon of unknown origin"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, nah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm…
I dunno if I want to upvote or downvote this one so no vote for now.
Will check back in a while for changes, while it is for sure better than it was it still needs a bit of tweaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview's a lot better. Good job taking out the diatribes while still making it abundantly clear what the guy's prejudices are. I do still think the "Method of Action" section should come before the discovery. And it does still feel longish (why not put the observation log behind a cut?), but the more I think about it, the more I'm liking it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I completely removed all the bigotry stuff (except for the interview, but put in elements implying he's deluded or a liar, as well as specifying that his racism is purely incidental to SCP-2299)

I hope not to alienate people who upvoted it as it was, but this version is much closer to my original-original old draft Cephalensis-8

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the horrors of true bigotry is that there is (usually) nothing to it beyond the instinctual need for humanity to classify everybody into easily-identifiable groups.

Take Dmitri, created by Waxx, for instance. Yeah, we find him funny, but if he was an actual person? He'd be a monster. No mental illness, no SCP involvement, just an otherwise normal person. Who goes on murderous rampages when he hears that Chechens are involved with something nearby.

By skipifying it, you take away some of that fridge horror. That YOU could become a monster for reasons that are well within you control.

But that's my opinion. As of this writing this SCP is at +5 with 28 people voting, so you've done something right. And controversial. Doing what I said might make it better, or drop this into downvote territory. Let other people weigh in before you change it. If you change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if the bigotry was only against the non-infected instead of falling to traditional minority lines, I'd be upvoting this in a heartbeat.

I was thinking of doing this (and in fact it was in Cephalensis-8) but I thought it might be too cliché =x

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read before changes, downvoted. Read after, still downvoted.

Essentially, this is a Thing What Makes You Bigoted. And, at least for me, its not done well enough to get away with that. Especially with the implications that it occurs because it causes brain damage.

Interestingly enough, if the bigotry was only against the non-infected instead of falling to traditional minority lines, I'd be upvoting this in a heartbeat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rewrote the latter half of the interview and removed much of the blatant "bigotry mode engaged!1" stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every single part of this article goes on for way too long. Most damning is how long it takes to actually figure out what the hell the anomaly does to people. The observation log felt unnecessary, and certainly off-tone enough as to be not very useful, and the interview, while illustrative, is also kind of not something I feel like reading at the end of a huge article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Much improved from the early draft - same basic idea, but much better writing.

I find myself asking though, what exactly is the effect of this Cthulu-like being? Does it take ordinary morons with their vague and confused prejudices and turn them into "Davids" - David Icke, David Duke, David Emory, or similar obsessed conspiracy theorists? Not really - they're crazy but focused and consistent, which the interview subject isn't.

Does it turn them into Fred Phelps - a man whose entire consciousness was warped around a single hatred, changing everything else to fit? Or does it turn them into Jesse Ventura or Alex Jones, men who never met a conspiracy theory they didn't like? Again, not really.

There's no breakdown of social order, or change in power structure. The people aren't deranged, or fanatical, just…hicks. Creationists, tea partiers, congregations of tiny evangelical chirches. It's like Stepford transposed to Iowa.

The interpretation of fictional events as real memories

Do we have a word for this? It's not "confabulation", but we certainly should have a term for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
People getting worked up into killing each other over stupid shit is not a terribly anomalous or really even uncommon human behavior. I get that the anomalous part is the spooky/random sky octopus thing causing it, but on several levels this feels like a cheapening of real-life conflict. A lot of the article's supposed weirdness stems from the fact that this happening in First World locales and is encouraging violence against groups like "people from Michigan", which I find kind of obnoxious ("look, it's Americans killing each other over ethnic/ideological/religious conflicts, how wacky is that, guys?").

Aside from that, you've buried the shit out of your lede. We don't find out why exactly this is anomalous or what it even does until waaaay down into the article. Frankly, the only reason I finished it was because I had seen some of the earlier comments and knew what was coming. The octopus-in-the-sky stuff feels exceedingly random and tacked-on since you don't really go anywhere with it, which to me hurts the article's flow since I'm looking for why the hell that matters as I'm reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not at all what I even suggested. It's more along the lines of "How many jelly beans are in this jar?" "A lot" vs "not many"—it's not exact, but it's pretty clearly obvious to an average person

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
> How does one measure charisma?

Easily. This is charismatic. This is not.

Measurement goes beyond the binary state of presence/absence. It's not just "How much hydrogen is there in the jar?" "Yes."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first "giant ghostly octopus" definitely worked. I think the second instance of ghostly in quotes was a little jarring. At that point I'd expect them to switch to a clearer nonquoted word. Translucent?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added an addendum more explicitly clarifying that this isn't a racism/sexism ghost-disease; those are merely incidental as a result of the alterations to the brain. People who are completely and utterly not racist or sexist or nationalistic or homophobic are still susceptible to this thing and liable to be extremely violent

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is great. Well written, particularly the interrogation which seems very real.
Two things: I think you mean the word 'resistant' instead of 'resilient'. Also, confabulation means something other than how you defined it.

Ghostly tentacles. That image had staying power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one gets it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I get that this isn't responsible for all hate groups/extremist organizations, but it still seems to be saying that hate/extremist groups are in and of themselves anomalous human behaviors. This may sound cynical, but I have difficulty believing that hatred of "the other" is anomalous to normal human behavior (as a whole population, rather than specific individuals).

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't get the feeling that it was trying to claim that all prejudice and hatred were "alien influences" - it felt to me more like the classic Twilight Zone episode, "The Monsters Are Due On Maple Street", where invading aliens let the pre-existing fears and bigotries of the humans do the work for them, with just a little nudge. I guess those who didn't survive the infection were that tiny minority of human beings who actually don't harbor any hidden prejudices.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just as an aside, "MTF-501" is not consistent with the normal naming convention for Mobile Task Forces.

I read over it twice and… I'm just not getting it. I'm going to have to echo the above concern about prejudice-apologism and add that I feel like it's trying to hint at something that could tie this all together but just doesn't ever pay out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While it's a unique concept… I'm not too sure I like it.

I don't like it either =\ I hope it doesn't come off too strongly

How does one measure charisma?

Easily. This is charismatic. This is not.

This implies it can be stopped with enough time

No, it means that new alterations stop, but the stuff already done is still done. Maybe I should reword that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I'm this close to getting it, and it's fascinatingly bizarre and chilling. Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, it's a mind virus caused by something like Cthulhu that causes xenophobia, or, as the article so eloquently puts it:

You think we all got infected by some "crazy mutant virus" that's turning us all into racist zombies attacking and killing each other.

While it's a unique concept… I'm not too sure I like it. To me, it's saying 'racism and genocide aren't because of mob mentality, ignorance and charismatic yet horrible people, it's the fault of a mind bug that makes you act horrible.' Don't get me wrong, I'm all for demonizing ignorance, but… bleh. I'm probably just reading too much into it, but SCPs that take blame off of the human condition just irk me.

although due to the highly charismatic nature of the affected individuals

How does one measure charisma? This isn't D&D, so I don't think that charisma can be put into a quantitative form, unless this is D&D with Order of the Stick rules.

Also, the article has a rather blatant contradiction:

After 30-36 hours, all major alterations of the affected individual's brain cease.

This implies it can be stopped with enough time. If that's the case, why hasn't it worn off in the original town? The containment procedures essentially say this is the case, so why bother putting in something that says it can wear off if it doesn't wear off?

The concept and writing are good, and the interview is spot-on, so this at least saves it from a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank HatMan and Marathon, DrYucatan, Voct, Vince, chubert

The interview was based on actual conversations I had with an actual person who actually said some of the things I've repeated in the interview. Since original posting, it's been edited pretty substantially so it's not as much of it.

Also, this is a very extremely heavily re-written version of the first SCP I ever attempted on the site, Cephalensis-8, which had basically the same symptoms, but clumsy execution and piss-poor foreshadowing/aftshadowing
